stories.title: Shorenstein, Portman Agree to Terms on Nashville Office Sale
stories.excerpt: Shorenstein Investment Advisers has purchased the Moore Building, a 245,826-square-foot office property in Nashville, Tenn. Portman Holdings was the seller. The acquisition comes on the heals of the company buying a 330,000 square foot building in the Gulch Union district for $217 million.  The social media company TikTok rents 143,000 square feet in the building.  The Moore Building is at 827 19th Ave. S., within Music Row. Portman delivered the 16-story building in 2023, along with Creed Investment Co. The office development features a fitness center, conference rooms, indoor and outdoor lounges and 8,500 square feet of retail space. This acquisition marks Shorenstein’s fourth Nashville office investment. The last big office sale occurred in May when \ Highwoods Properties sold Bridgestone Tower, a 522,000-square-foot building, for $255 million or $488.5 per square foot. The post Shorenstein, Portman Agree to Terms on Nashville Office Sale appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Shorenstein acquires Moore Building, 245,826 SF Nashville office with TikTok as 143K SF tenant.
Fourth Nashville office investment for Shorenstein; follows $217M Gulch Union acquisition.
Recent comparable: Highwoods sold Bridgestone Tower (522K SF) for $488.50/SF in May.
stories.summary: Shorenstein Investment Advisers purchased the 16-story Moore Building (245,826 SF) in Nashville's Music Row from Portman Holdings, marking the firm's fourth Nashville office acquisition. TikTok occupies 143,000 SF of the space. The deal follows Shorenstein's $217 million acquisition of a 330,000 SF building in the Gulch Union district. Market context: Highwoods Properties sold Bridgestone Tower (522,000 SF) for $255 million ($488.50/SF) in May 2024.
stories.impact: Office sales to institutional capital in Nashville signal stabilizing valuations and investor appetite despite broader office headwinds. For multifamily developers, this suggests confidence in Nashville's employment and population growth trajectory, supporting continued demand for rental housing in employment corridors and urban-core submarkets like Music Row and Gulch.

stories.title: CoreWeave Doubles Headquarters Space to 62K SF
stories.excerpt: CoreWeave has signed a 31,000-square-foot, long-term expansion of its office space at Eastman Companies’ The Eisenhower at 290 W. Mount Pleasant Ave. in Livingston, NJ, less than a year after its initial 31,000-square-foot lease. The rapidly growing artificial intelligence (AI) company houses its corporate headquarters at the site. The CBRE team of Jon Meisel, Erin Wenzler and Dan Casey with Eric Maurer of Eastman Companies represented the landlord in leasing negotiations. CoreWeave was represented by Jamie Ragucci, Kevin Murphy and Aaron Sommer of Newmark “There were no other competitive buildings in the Livingston submarket that could deliver on all of CoreWeave’s needs, including an expansive list of on-site amenities, 10+ conference rooms and auditorium, back-up generator, walkable retail, building signage and space to expand,” said Wenzler. “In addition to top-quality office space, the AI firm was also drawn to the property’s steady and stable ownership.” . The post CoreWeave Doubles Headquarters Space to 62K SF appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CoreWeave doubles HQ footprint to 62K SF at Eisenhower, Livingston, NJ in under 12 months.
AI company expansion driven by amenities, conference capacity, backup power, and limited submarket alternatives.
No competitive buildings in Livingston could meet all CoreWeave requirements.
stories.summary: CoreWeave, a rapidly growing AI company, expanded its headquarters at The Eisenhower in Livingston, NJ by signing an additional 31,000 SF lease, doubling its footprint to 62,000 SF in less than one year. The expansion was motivated by facility requirements including 10+ conference rooms, auditorium, backup generator, walkable retail, and limited competitive alternatives in the submarket. Landlord Eastman Companies and brokerage CBRE facilitated the transaction.
stories.impact: CoreWeave's aggressive expansion in Livingston signals sustained AI/compute industry hiring and capital deployment in the New Jersey market, a secondary hub for tech talent and infrastructure. For multifamily developers in North Jersey (especially near Livingston, Newark, and Jersey City), this validates near-term demand growth from high-wage tech workers relocating or commuting from Manhattan; developers should prioritize acquisitions or entitlements in walkable, transit-adjacent nodes near employment clusters.

stories.title: Ariel Closes Sale of Upper West Side Section 8 Apartments
stories.excerpt: Ariel Property Advisors on Monday announced the $75-million sale of Manhattan Valley Apartments, a two-building multifamily portfolio located at 200 Manhattan Ave. and 133 W. 104th St. in Manhattan Valley on the Upper West Side. Founding partner Victor Sozio, president and founder Shimon Shkury and director of research and sales Remi Mandell arranged the sale on behalf of the seller. The buyer was not disclosed. The portfolio encompasses 143,551 gross square feet and consists of 126 Project-Based Section 8 voucher units. Centrally located in Manhattan Valley, the buildings offer streamlined management and operational efficiencies, according to Ariel. Located between Central Park West and Broadway, the properties offer immediate access to Central Park as well as the neighborhood’s array of restaurants, retail destinations, and other attractions. Ariel said the area is known for its tree-lined streets, classic brownstones and mid-rise residential buildings, making it one of Manhattan’s most sought-after residential areas. The post Ariel Closes Sale of Upper West Side Section 8 Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Ariel closes $75M sale of 126-unit Section 8 portfolio on Upper West Side Manhattan.
Manhattan Valley Apartments: 143,551 gross SF, two buildings, project-based vouchers, buyer undisclosed.
Located between Central Park West and Broadway; premium location with strong operational profile.
stories.summary: Ariel Property Advisors arranged the $75 million sale of Manhattan Valley Apartments, a two-building, 126-unit Section 8 voucher portfolio on the Upper West Side (200 Manhattan Ave. and 133 W. 104th St.). The 143,551 gross SF properties include project-based Section 8 units and operate with streamlined management. The buyer was not disclosed.
stories.impact: Sale of stabilized, federally-subsidized multifamily in prime Manhattan location at strong valuation signals sustained institutional and family office demand for de-risked affordable housing with long-term rental income. For Trammell Crow and peers, this underscores the value premium and capital attraction of mixed-income or workforce housing in constrained, high-cost metros where affordable supply is scarce—a market segment less vulnerable to rent compression and economic cycles.

stories.title: 45-Unit Garden Apartment Property Trades in LA’s Palms Neighborhood
stories.excerpt: Northmarq’s Los Angeles Investment Sales team of Mike Hanassab, Steven Goldstein and Elliot Hassan arranged the $13.125 million sale of 3717 Cardiff Ave., a 45-unit garden apartment community located in the Palms neighborhood of Los Angeles, near Culver City. Northmarq represented the seller, a family that had owned the property since the mid-1970s, and the buyer, a local private investor. “Cardiff represented a unique buying opportunity in a submarket that rarely offers 40-plus-unit properties for sale,” said Goldstein. “Given the rarity of the opportunity, our marketing process generated over 20 private tours and double-digit offers, with several non-refundable at the opening of escrow. The surrounding submarket is lined with institutional capital, which gave the buyer confidence in continued demand and the neighborhood’s overall desirability.” Built in 1973, 3717 Cardiff Ave. is a three-story multifamily community consisting of 31 one-bedroom and 14 two-bedroom apartment homes. Units feature oversized floor plans, in-unit fireplaces and private balconies in most units. The post 45-Unit Garden Apartment Property Trades in LA’s Palms Neighborhood appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: 45-unit garden apartment, 3717 Cardiff Ave., Palms/Culver City LA trades for $13.125M.
Built 1973: 31 one-BR, 14 two-BR units; oversized plans, in-unit fireplaces, private balconies.
20+ tours, double-digit offers generated; rare offering in submarket lined with institutional capital.
stories.summary: Northmarq arranged the $13.125 million sale of a 45-unit garden apartment community in LA's Palms neighborhood near Culver City, built in 1973 and owned by a family since the mid-1970s. The three-story property comprises 31 one-bedroom and 14 two-bedroom units with oversized floor plans and in-unit fireplaces. The transaction generated over 20 private tours and double-digit competitive offers.
stories.impact: Scarcity of 40+ unit multifamily assets in the Palms submarket combined with competitive bidding and institutional capital presence indicates strong absorption and investor confidence in the LA market. For developers, this signals limited new supply relative to demand, supporting rent growth; the vintage garden-style product and premium valuation suggest acquisition or development of comparable mid-rise infill projects in West LA submarkets with limited inventory turnover could command strong pricing.

stories.title: Caribbean Restaurant Concept Brings Everett’s Gateway Center to 100% Leased
stories.excerpt: Urban Edge Properties said Monday that New York-based Juici Patties, a fast-growing Caribbean restaurant concept, will open at Gateway Center in Everett, MA. The 2,450-square-foot space will be the brand’s first location in Massachusetts. “By helping to launch this new dining experience in the Boston area, Urban Edge continues to upgrade the merchandise mix at Gateway Center, bringing it to 100% leased,” said Ronnie Bertrand, director of leasing for Urban Edge. “Juici Patties brings a new menu to the center and community and we’re excited to be a part of their expansion.” Bertrand represented ownership in leasing negotiations. Juici Patties was represented by Michael Nolan of CBRE. The restaurant menu includes traditional Jamaican patties made of beef, chicken, or spinach, along with plantain and potato fries. Caribbean flavors are also represented in the drinks and sides. Gateway Center’s other tenants include Home Depot, Mattress Firm, Target and Costco. The post Caribbean Restaurant Concept Brings Everett’s Gateway Center to 100% Leased appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Juici Patties (Caribbean concept) leases 2,450 SF at Gateway Center, Everett, MA; brings property to 100% occupancy.
Urban Edge Properties co-tenants: Home Depot, Target, Costco, Mattress Firm.
First Massachusetts location for NYC-based restaurant chain; indicates retail demand and trade-area desirability.
stories.summary: Urban Edge Properties announced that Juici Patties, a New York-based Caribbean restaurant concept, will occupy 2,450 SF at Gateway Center in Everett, MA, achieving 100% occupancy at the shopping center. The lease marks the brand's first Massachusetts expansion. Co-tenants include Home Depot, Target, Costco, and Mattress Firm.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Retail leasing at a grocery-anchored suburban center reflects general commercial health but does not directly signal housing demand, employment growth, or population migration into Everett. However, strong retail tenancy and anchor stability can support multifamily asset valuations in transit-accessible or employment-adjacent submarkets by confirming trade-area vitality.

stories.title: Former Downtown Brooklyn Macy’s Slated for Dramatic Retail Makeover
stories.excerpt: The 440,000-square-foot space at Downtown Brooklyn’s 422 Fulton Street — the largest block of retail space currently available in New York City and the former site of a Macy’s department store — will become BKX, a five-floor retail and amusement destination that’s being described by developers as a “next-generation cultural and commercial hub,” Commercial Observer has learned. Plans for BKX were shared exclusively with Commercial Observer by one of the property’s owners, United American Land, in conjunction with co-owner the Jackson Group and retail brokerage and consultancy Dreamscape Retail & Entertainment, which is spearheading the conversion efforts, including tenant recruitment. “When we acquired this property, we saw an opportunity to reimagine one of New York’s most iconic sites for the next generation,” Albert Laboz, a principal of United American Land, said in the announcement. “Rather than pursuing a traditional retail redevelopment, we’re creating a destination that reflects how people want to spend time today — bringing together entertainment, dining, retail and community under one roof. BKX represents our belief in Downtown Brooklyn and its future as one of New York City’s most dynamic mixed-use neighborhoods, where people come to live, work, learn and explore.” The company described the type of tenants the building will house as “flagship retailers, entertainment concepts, immersive attractions, food and beverage experiences and next-generation brands seeking a highly visible urban presence.” Dreamscape’s experiential and entertainment roster includes projects at South Street Seaport, including Pier 17; One Wall Street; the Arcade Nashville; and Rio Las Vegas, a casino resort. The company is working on BKX with experiential design firm iCrave, which has worked on projects such as the TSX Broadway mixed-use tower in New York and the Sphere entertainment venue in Las Vegas. The project will be the first of its kind in New York City, said Joshua
bullets: 440K SF former Macy's at 422 Fulton Street, Downtown Brooklyn converting to BKX mixed retail/amusement.
Largest contiguous retail block available in NYC; five-floor 'cultural and commercial hub' redevelopment.
Owned by United American Land and Jackson Group; Dreamscape Retail spearheading tenant recruitment.
stories.summary: The 440,000 SF former Macy's building at Downtown Brooklyn's 422 Fulton Street—the largest block of retail space currently available in NYC—will be redeveloped as BKX, a five-floor retail and amusement destination marketed as a "next-generation cultural and commercial hub." United American Land, Jackson Group, and Dreamscape Retail & Entertainment are driving the conversion and tenant recruitment.
stories.impact: Conversion of a major department store anchor to mixed-use retail/entertainment in Downtown Brooklyn reinforces the neighborhood's repositioning as an urban lifestyle and commerce destination. For multifamily developers, this validates continued demand for rental housing within walking distance of Downtown Brooklyn's employment, transit, and entertainment amenities; the retail redevelopment should support net migration of younger, affluent renters and justify premium pricing for nearby residential projects.

stories.title: PGIM Sells Coral Gables Office Complex for $98M
stories.excerpt: Insurance giant PGIM has sold the 717,805-square-foot Ponce office complex in Coral Gables, Fla., for $97.8 million. The buyers include Intalex, Itero, Greenwall Capital Management and the family office of the late entrepreneur Carl DeSantis, who founded Sundown Vitamins and was an early investor in the Celsius energy drink. (He was not related to Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis.) The asset, which is 65 percent leased, includes four structures: a 257,168-square-foot Mediterranean-style office building at 2525 Ponce de Leon Boulevard; an adjoining, 93,670-square-feet office building, which is attached to a six-story parking garage at 2555 Ponce de Leon Boulevard; and a three-story parking garage across the street at 152 Valencia Avenue. Acore Capital provided a $105 million acquisition loan. The partnership plans to spend about $30 million renovating the complex, which was built between 1966 and 2004. The project is expected to be done next year. “Premier assets in high-growth, supply-constrained markets continue to outperform,” Jack Henry Kapp, managing partner of Greenwall, said in a statement. “Even prior to closing, our pre-leasing activity has put us well ahead of schedule in executing our business plan.” PGIM purchased the property for an undisclosed amount 20 years ago from Hines, which had developed and repositioned the Ponce. Hines will continue to continue to manage the asset. Chris Lee, Sean Kelly, Tom Rappa, and Matthew Lee of CBRE represented the seller, and Amy Julian and Andrew Chilgren of CBRE arranged the financing. A representative for PGIM did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The deal follows another repositioning sale of an office building in Coral Gables, an upscale suburb of Miami. In January, Deutsche Bank’s asset management arm sold off the Alhambra for $120 million, about the same amount it paid more than a decade ago, to private equity firms Lone Star Funds, Square2 Capital and Highline Real Estate Capital. Julia Echikson ca
bullets: PGIM sells Ponce office complex, Coral Gables, FL (717,805 SF, 65% leased) for $97.8M.
Buyers: Intalex, Itero, Greenwall Capital, Carl DeSantis family office; four-building portfolio.
Purchasers plan $30M renovation; 65% occupancy and fixed-rate financing support value-add thesis.
stories.summary: PGIM sold the 717,805 SF Ponce office complex in Coral Gables, FL (four buildings across 2525 and 2555 Ponce de Leon Boulevard and 152 Valencia Avenue) for $97.8 million at 65% occupancy. Buyers include Intalex, Itero, Greenwall Capital Management, and the family office of Carl DeSantis. Acore Capital provided a $105 million acquisition loan; the partnership plans $30 million in renovations with completion expected in 2025.
stories.impact: Sale of a mid-occupancy office complex with significant capital earmarked for repositioning signals that Coral Gables remains attractive to value-add capital despite broader office headwinds. For multifamily developers in Miami/Coral Gables, this underscores continued institutional confidence in the market's demographic appeal and job growth potential; the influx of capital and renovation activity in office submarkets should support continued residential demand from white-collar workers and justify new multifamily development in walkable, mixed-use nodes.

stories.title: Law Firm Loeb & Loeb Expands to 179K SF at Rudin’s 345 Park Avenue
stories.excerpt: A global law firm is growing its office footprint to 178,959 square feet in Midtown East. Loeb & Loeb, which has more than 450 attorneys across the U.S. and Asia, signed a 16-year extension for its existing 160,051-square-foot offices on the 18th through 21st floors of Rudin’s 345 Park Avenue and added 18,908 square feet on part of the 13th floor, according to the landlord. Loeb & Loeb has leased its New York City headquarters at the building since 1993. Rudin did not disclose the asking rent for the building, but the average office asking rent in Midtown was $86.55 per square foot in May, according to a report from CBRE. “Loeb & Loeb has been a valued part of the 345 Park Avenue community for more than 30 years, and we take great pride in extending and expanding our relationship through the end of the next decade,” Michael Rudin, co-CEO of Rudin, said in a statement. “Our continued investment in 345 Park to ensure it remains a top corporate destination in the heart of the Plaza District was a key factor in their leasing decision.” Lewis Miller, Ken Rapp, Michael Wellen and Alex Kucera of CBRE negotiated on behalf of Loeb & Loeb, while Robert Steinman handled the deal on behalf of the landlord in-house. CBRE did not immediately respond to a request for comment. 345 Park Avenue spans a full block bordered by Park and Lexington avenues and East 51st and East 52nd streets. The building was the site of a mass shooting in July 2025 that killed four, including two real estate professionals. Other tenants include French chef Cyril Lignac, who signed a 22,000-square-foot lease for a Franco-Asian restaurant concept in April, and East West Bank, which signed a 38,000-square-foot lease in February. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Loeb & Loeb expands to 178,959 SF at Rudin's 345 Park Avenue, Midtown East, 16-year extension.
Law firm adds 18,908 SF (13th floor) to existing 160,051 SF (floors 18-21); tenant since 1993.
Midtown office asking rent averaging $86.55/SF (May 2024); expansion signals continued CBD occupancy demand.
stories.summary: Loeb & Loeb, a global law firm with 450+ attorneys, signed a 16-year extension and expansion at Rudin's 345 Park Avenue in Midtown East, growing from 160,051 SF to 178,959 SF by adding 18,908 SF on the 13th floor. The firm has anchored the building since 1993. Midtown office asking rents averaged $86.55/SF as of May 2024.
stories.impact: Long-term lease extension and expansion by a major law firm at a premier Midtown office tower demonstrates continued tenant demand and landlord confidence in the Manhattan CBD despite post-pandemic office challenges. For multifamily developers in Midtown East and Plaza District submarkets, this validates sustained professional-services employment and white-collar worker density; new residential projects with premium amenities targeting finance/law professionals remain justified in Manhattan's core employment corridors.

stories.title: Barry LePatner of LePatner & Associates: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: Barry LePatner, founder of the New York City-based law firm LePatner & Associates, has kept his finger on the pulse of the construction industry’s woes for over four decades. LePatner’s legal practice began more than 45 years ago representing architects, engineers and other designers in their work as a business and legal adviser. The firm grew quickly, developing an expertise in how projects are built, designed and negotiated. In the early `90s, the practice expanded to cater to real estate owners, developers and construction lenders. LePatner has been on a crusade to identify and eliminate inefficiencies in the construction process. In 2007, he authored Broken Buildings, Busted Budgets: How to Fix America’s Trillion-Dollar Construction Industry, advocating for the end of “fast-track” projects that incur cost overruns. That was followed in 2010 by his book on the state of aging U.S. infrastructure, Too Big to Fall: America’s Failing Infrastructure and the Way Forward. A 2008 New York Magazine article even dubbed him “a Cassandra of infrastructure.” Today, LePatner is echoing the ardent request of so many other professional industries: Use more technology. LePatner sees modern technological tools like artificial intelligence as a golden ticket to bypassing the construction industry’s chronic problems, like timeline delays, cost overruns and paperwork headaches. He says, however, that architects and designers don’t seem ready to adapt. Commercial Observer sat down with LePatner in June to discuss his views on the sources of the construction industry’s current headaches, and the tools that might help alleviate them. The following conversation has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: After 45 years working with architects, designers and developers, what concerns you most about the industry today? Barry LePatner: You’ll recall we started this century with a technological recession. Tech really went bottom up, construction projects stopped, a
bullets: Barry LePatner (LePatner & Associates) profiled; 45+ years advising architects, engineers, developers, lenders.
Founder advocates for construction efficiency, eliminating fast-track projects, and regulatory reform.
Expertise: project delivery, cost control, financing, and construction-industry systemic inefficiencies.
stories.summary: Barry LePatner, founder of New York-based law firm LePatner & Associates, has spent 45+ years advising architects, engineers, developers, and construction lenders. His practice focuses on project delivery, cost controls, and construction-industry inefficiencies. LePatner authored "Broken Buildings, Busted Budgets" (2007) and "Too Big to Fall" (2010), advocating for elimination of fast-track projects and regulatory reform.
stories.impact: Profile of a construction-law thought leader underscores ongoing industry challenges around cost inflation, schedule delays, and inefficient delivery models—critical risk factors for multifamily developers. Developers should heed LePatner's warnings about fast-tracking and prioritize disciplined project management, fixed-price contracting, and phased financing to mitigate construction-cost volatility in an inflationary environment.

stories.title: EMP Capital Group Plans to Build 176 Units in Astoria as Part of Mega-Development
stories.excerpt: EMP Capital Group is forging ahead with its plan to bring new residential buildings to Astoria, Queens. The real estate investment and development company has filed two new plans with the New York City Department of Buildings calling for the construction of a 99-unit building at 35-13 Steinway Street and a 77-unit residential property next door at 35-08 41st Street. Elichai Pariente, founder of EMP Capital, and registered architect Ralph Kowalczyk of Issac & Stern Architects were listed as the applicants on both plans. Spokespeople for Pariente and Kowalczyk did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s requests for comment. If approved, the 102,300-square-foot residential building at 35-13 Steinway Street would include retail space, a fitness center, a theater and outdoor recreation space, according to the DOB plans. Plans also call for between five and six residential units on the third through 19th floors. Meanwhile, the 81,576-square-foot residential building at 35-08 41St Street, if approved, would include recreation and retail space, as well as a pet spa and a designated amenity lounge on the 14th and 15th floors. There would be between three and five residential units on floors three through 13, and three units each on the 16th through 26th floors. There would also be outdoor space on the building’s roof, according to the DOB plans. News of the filings come after EMP Capital previously submitted plans in May to build a pair of 99-unit residential buildings at 35-43 37th Street and 37-09 36th Avenue in Astoria, Crain’s New York Business reported at the time. EMP Capital also filed a proposal last month to construct a 79-unit apartment building at 40-16 35th Avenue, PincusCo reported. All of the proposed projects — within roughly three blocks of each other — would comprise more than 450 new housing units. Many local developers are filing plans for 99-unit buildings in the wake of the much-debated 485-x program, which provides tax incentives for devel
bullets: EMP Capital Group files plans for 176 units across two buildings in Astoria, Queens.
99-unit building at 35-13 Steinway Street (102,300 SF); 77-unit building at 35-08 41st Street (81,576 SF).
Buildings include retail, fitness, theater, pet spa, recreation, parking; 5-6 units per floor planned.
stories.summary: EMP Capital Group filed Department of Buildings applications for two new residential buildings in Astoria, Queens totaling 176 units: a 99-unit, 102,300 SF building at 35-13 Steinway Street and a 77-unit, 81,576 SF building at 35-08 41st Street. Both projects include retail, recreation, fitness, theater, pet amenities, and parking. Elichai Pariente (EMP founder) and architect Ralph Kowalczyk are listed applicants.
stories.impact: New multifamily filings in Astoria signal continued developer confidence in Queens' residential fundamentals and zoning approvals. EMP's dual-building strategy with substantial amenity packages (fitness, theater, retail) indicates competitive brand positioning in a market with institutional development activity. For Trammell Crow and peers, Astoria's transit connectivity to Manhattan and growing employment base justify new-build or value-add acquisition strategies; monitor zoning approval pace and comparable rent trends to validate development economics.

stories.title: Azorim secures financing for final phase of Yonkers complex
stories.excerpt: An Israel-based development firm is preparing to put the finishing touches on a massive multifamily complex a dozen miles north of Manhattan. Azorim landed $69 million in construction financing from Western Alliance Bank for the fourth and final building of Mizora at Ridge Hill in Yonkers, the Commercial Observer reported. This component of the master-planned community in the Westchester County city is at 599 Ridge Hill Boulevard. The financing will support work on a 14-story, 174-unit building, dubbed Mizora Tower 4 (Mizora is “Azorim” spelled backward). There will also be 248 parking spaces as part of this phase of the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Azorim secures $69M construction financing from Western Alliance Bank for Mizora Tower 4, Yonkers.
174-unit, 14-story building at 599 Ridge Hill Boulevard; includes 248 parking spaces.
Completes 520-unit master-planned community; PILOT and market-rate bond structure optimized financing.
stories.summary: Azorim, an Israel-based developer, secured $69 million in fixed-rate, interest-only construction financing from Western Alliance Bank for Mizora Tower 4, the fourth and final building of the Mizora at Ridge Hill master-planned community in Yonkers. The 14-story, 174-unit building at 599 Ridge Hill Boulevard includes 248 parking spaces. Completion of all four phases will total 520 units connected by a 25,000 SF amenity center with pool, basketball court, movie theater, and track.
stories.impact: Successful construction financing for the final phase of a large Westchester multifamily development despite "ongoing market headwinds" signals lender confidence in Yonkers' rental demand and Azorim's execution track record. For multifamily developers in the tri-state, this validates the Yonkers market as a secondary growth hub for spillover demand from Manhattan; the transit-oriented location and completed phases should support strong absorption and rent growth, supporting continued development in Westchester's job-rich corridors near Hudson River transit.

stories.title: Avery Hall plots 250 units at stalled Crown Heights hotel site
stories.excerpt: One year on from purchasing the site out of bankruptcy, Avery Hall Investments is revealing the future for a Crown Heights lot once envisioned for a hotel. The Brooklyn-based developer filed an application with the New York City Department of Buildings for a mixed-use project on a vacant parcel at 1550 Bedford Avenue, the Commercial Observer reported. Through its land use application, Avery Hall is petitioning for zoning amendments to permit a mix of residential and commercial use, along with a mandatory inclusionary housing area designation for affordable housing. Avery Hall plans to build a 173,000-square-foot building with 250 units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Avery Hall files plans for 250-unit mixed-use building at 1550 Bedford Avenue, Crown Heights, Brooklyn.
173K SF, 18-story development; 75 units designated affordable (MIH); retail on ground floor.
Site purchased from Yoel Goldman out of bankruptcy for $10.1M; zoning amendments required.
stories.summary: Avery Hall Investments filed an application with NYC Department of Buildings for a 173,000 SF, 18-story mixed-use building at 1550 Bedford Avenue in Crown Heights containing 250 units (75 designated affordable under mandatory inclusionary housing), ground-floor retail, and seeking zoning amendments. Avery Hall purchased the vacant parcel from Yoel Goldman out of bankruptcy in August 2024 for $10.1 million. The site was previously approved for a 100-key hotel designed by ODA but stalled during the pandemic.
stories.impact: Conversion of a stalled hotel site to multifamily with significant affordable housing component reflects NYC's ongoing residential demand and developer appetite for MIH projects with mixed-income economics. For multifamily developers in Brooklyn, this validates the market's zoning flexibility and lender support for affordable-inclusive projects; monitor Crown Heights absorption and MIH rent caps to assess pricing and yield implications for future developments in central Brooklyn submarkets with similar regulatory profiles.

stories.title: Abbott’s shift into reverse puts data center debate into overdrive
stories.excerpt: Texas’ typically pro-business governor did an about-face on data centers amid growing public sentiment against the resource-intensive developments.  Less than a year ago, Gov. Greg Abbott lauded Texas as “the epicenter of AI development” when Google announced a $40 billion investment in November, the Texas Tribune reported.  He changed his tune this week when he called for an outright ban on data centers in rural areas during a campaign stop in Bullard, per the outlet. The announcement built on a letter Abbott sent to the Texas Legislature in June when he called for regulating data center development in Texas. Specifically, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Texas Gov. Greg Abbott reverses position on data centers; calls for rural ban, regulation after supporting AI.
Amid public opposition to noise, water, power usage; data center construction exceeded $50B nationally (April 2024).
San Marcos becomes first Texas city to attempt ban; legal challenges under HB 2559 expected.
stories.summary: Texas Governor Greg Abbott abruptly reversed his pro-data-center stance, calling for a ban on data center development in rural areas and stricter state regulation (annual reporting, limited incentives, closed-loop water systems). The shift follows mounting public opposition to noise, water consumption, and power demands from data center facilities. Data center construction nationwide exceeded $50 billion in April 2024. San Marcos became the first Texas city to attempt a data center ban, facing legal challenges under HB 2559.
stories.impact: Regulatory uncertainty and public backlash against data center expansion in Texas create both risk and opportunity for multifamily developers. If data center growth slows or consolidates to urban cores, job creation and in-migration patterns may shift away from rural and secondary markets toward major metros (Austin, Dallas, Houston). Developers should monitor state-level policy and city-level bans closely; concentrated data center activity in urban-core areas could boost demand for workforce and market-rate housing in tech-heavy submarkets, but speculative rural development risk has increased materially.
